comments.title: Ambiguous spatial conditioning for "in between" heuristic
comments.quote: Table 1: "Obj1 in between Obj2, Obj3" with criterion "min(_O_2_x, O_3_x_) _< O_1_x < max(_O_2_x, O_3_x_)".
comments.explanation: The heuristic only checks the x-coordinate (left/right) of the objects. For a 2D image, an object being 'in between' two others typically also requires a vertical (y) constraint; otherwise, an object that is far above or below the other two could still satisfy the x-range condition. The authors may assume that all objects lie on the same horizontal plane (e.g., a tabletop) so that y is irrelevant, but this is not stated. Without clarifying whether the task always keeps objects at similar y coordinates, the heuristic could generate false positive rewards.

comments.title: Overclaim: Full Reward Model performing 'almost as well as oracle reward'
comments.quote: Figure 6: We showcase our Full Reward Model performing almost as well as oracle reward on SawyerSimRobotPushing (pushing a blue puck close to a yellow puck), FetchSimRobot-Stacking (stacking a yellow block on top of a red block), and FetchSimRobot-Placing (placing a yellow block on the right of a red block).
comments.explanation: The caption claims the Full Reward Model performs 'almost as well as oracle reward.' However, the passage does not present quantitative results for these specific tasks in Figure 6; it only describes that the tasks were trained and compares them qualitatively. The actual numeric results are only presented later in Table 2 for the multi-task policy (which uses oracle reward as the evaluation metric, not the ZSRM reward). Without seeing the actual plot or quantitative comparison for the single-task training, the claim is too strong and could mislead a reader into believing the gap is negligible. The authors should either show the data or soften the language.

comments.title: Incorrect or ambiguous object color in task description
comments.quote: Our base reward model fails for pushing, stacking, and placing, which take "an image of a yellow block on top of a red block", "an image of a yellow object close to a blue object", and "an image of a yellow block on the right of a red block" as language input for those tasks respectively.
comments.explanation: There is an inconsistency in the object colors described. The pushing task is described earlier as 'pushing a blue puck close to a yellow puck' (in Figure 6 caption), but here the base reward model failure description for pushing says 'an image of a yellow object close to a blue object'—reversing the colors. The stacking and placing descriptions use a yellow block and a red block, consistent with Figure 6. This mismatch could confuse a reader about which exact goal text was used for the pushing task. It is likely a minor oversight, but it introduces ambiguity about the experimental condition.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to Figure 5 for image observation examples
comments.quote: See Figs. 3 & 5 for image observation examples of FetchSimRobot and SawyerSimRobot.
comments.explanation: Figure 5 is described earlier as showing base reward model outputs (dot product trends), not example images of the environments. The reader might be misled into thinking Figure 5 contains example observations, when in fact it contains reward curves. The authors likely intended to reference Figure 3 (which shows Grad-CAM outputs) and possibly a separate figure not included in this passage. This is a minor cross-reference error.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of method's state specification limitations
comments.quote: Our current method also does not specify state of the system other than semantic position such as object pose or closed door.
comments.explanation: The sentence is syntactically ambiguous. The phrase 'other than semantic position such as object pose or closed door' could be read either as (a) the method does not specify state except for semantic position, with 'object pose' and 'closed door' given as examples of that semantic position (which is odd, since object pose includes orientation and 'closed door' is a binary state, not a position), or (b) the method does not specify state beyond semantic position, and examples of unspecified state are object pose and closed door. The intended meaning is likely (b), but the phrasing is confusing and could lead a careful reader to incorrectly infer that the method handles orientation or door state. The context of the paper (Table 1, pixel-coordinate heuristics) clarifies that only relative spatial positions are used, so this is a minor wording flaw rather than a substantive error, but it should be rephrased for clarity.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling framework for zero-shot reward specification by leveraging CLIP for semantic grounding and incorporating spatial relationship rules to construct rewards from natural language descriptions, ultimately distilling a multi-task policy for robotic manipulation. The motivation is well-articulated, effectively highlighting the limitations of state-dependent rewards and the brittleness of image-based goals, while the proposed factored approach (separating 'what' vs. 'where/how') offers a promising strategy to mitigate CLIP's known deficits in spatial reasoning. The writing is generally clear, professional, and structured logically. However, there are significant issues that require attention. A critical terminological contradiction arises between the stated assumption of "no access to state" and the architecture's inclusion of a "State Extractor," creating ambiguity regarding whether the system relies on engine states or purely visual representations; this distinction is vital for claiming zero-shot generalization. Furthermore, the introduction is vague regarding the implementation of the spatial grounding module; given that CLIP performs poorly on spatial reasoning, the authors must clarify how the "where/how" component robustly resolves spatial constraints from raw pixels without auxiliary annotations or access to ground-truth state information. Finally, the "zero-shot" claim should be precisely qualified to distinguish between zero-shot reward derivation and the training required for policy distillation, ensuring that the generalization capabilities of the distilled policy are accurately characterized relative to the training distribution.

comments.title: Missing article in list
comments.quote: algorithms which learn optimal policies that maximize expected reward can learn to perform navigation, dexterous manipulation, and host of other impactful tasks.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly frame the broad appeal of reinforcement learning and its capacity to learn diverse tasks. However, the phrase 'and host of other' is grammatically incomplete. It requires the definite article 'a' to read smoothly as 'and a host of other'. This is a minor grammatical oversight that does not affect the technical meaning but slightly disrupts the flow of the introduction.

comments.title: Incorrect phrasing for cross-referencing
comments.quote: Surprisingly, this can work in simple cases, for examples see the top example in Figure 5.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'for examples see' is awkward and likely a typo. Given the subsequent reference to 'the top example' (singular), the authors clearly intend to cite a single illustrative case. The standard academic convention here would be 'for example, see'. I initially wondered if the authors meant to reference multiple examples across Figure 5, but the singular reference clarifies this is just an editing slip that should be corrected for professionalism.

comments.title: Underspecified saliency computation for custom text embeddings
comments.quote: We use the language encodings as class embeddings of each object noun phrase to perform a saliency analysis (using Grad-CAM (Selvaraju et al., 2017)) on the image encoding of the observation on the last convolutional layer
comments.explanation: Standard Grad-CAM requires a differentiable target score to compute gradients with respect to feature maps. The passage mentions using language encodings as 'class embeddings' but does not specify the actual scoring function used for backpropagation. In CLIP-based open-vocabulary grounding, this typically involves computing the dot product or cosine similarity between the image encoder's patch features and the text embedding, then backpropagating through the image encoder. Without explicitly stating how the target loss is constructed, a reader cannot fully reproduce the gradient flow. This is a minor underspecification but affects technical clarity.

comments.title: Incomplete coordinate check for 'in between' heuristic
comments.quote: |Obj1 in between Obj2, Obj3|_min_(_O_2 _x, O_3 _x_) _< O_1 _x < max_(_O_2 _x, O_3 _x_)|
comments.explanation: The reward criteria for 'Obj1 in between Obj2, Obj3' only constrains the x-coordinate. The preceding paragraph explicitly defines a 2D coordinate system with positive y upward and positive x to the right, which is standard for table-top manipulation. In 2D space, satisfying the x-constraint alone does not guarantee Obj1 is spatially 'between' the other two objects, as it could be vertically far away or misaligned in y. The authors should either clarify if objects are constrained to a 1D rail or update the heuristic to include a y-coordinate proximity check.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimensionality claim vs. table definitions
comments.quote: The oracle reward uses true x,y,z spatial positions of the objects to determine if the desired spatial relationship is reached
comments.explanation: The text claims the oracle reward uses x, y, and z coordinates. However, Table 1 and all subsequent heuristic definitions only reference x, y, and x2 coordinates. The z-coordinate is never used or defined in the reward criteria. This suggests a minor inconsistency in the text (likely intended to say x,y) or that the 3D aspect was omitted from the formal definitions. Given the context of 2D table manipulation, 2D coordinates are sufficient, making the 'z' mention a localized error.

comments.title: Typographical error in Section 2.3
comments.quote: We then create a large dataset of the rollouts of those polices and pair each trajectory with the goal text description
comments.explanation: The word 'policies' is misspelled as 'polices'. This is a straightforward typo that should be corrected for professional presentation.

comments.title: Phrasing contradicts text-conditioned premise
comments.quote: Our base reward model fails for pushing, stacking, and placing, which take “an image of a yellow block on top of a red block”, “an image of a yellow object close to a blue object”, and “an image of a yellow block on the right of a red block” as language input for those tasks respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the tasks take 'an image of...' as language input. This directly contradicts the paper's core premise of using *goal-text descriptions* (natural language) rather than images for task specification. The authors clearly intended to quote the *goal text descriptions* themselves (e.g., 'a yellow block on top of a red block') but accidentally retained 'an image of...' from a previous draft or mental model. As written, it misleads readers into thinking the reward model expects visual inputs for these tasks, which undermines the zero-shot text-conditioned contribution.

comments.title: "By chance" phrasing undermines subsequent speculation
comments.quote: For Double inverted pendulum (Fig. 4) our base reward model does better than oracle by chance which we speculate is because the oracle reward was originally designed for state input and was not tuned for learning image to reward mapping.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'by chance' typically implies statistical noise, accidental outcome, or lack of systematic causality. However, the sentence immediately follows with 'which we speculate is because...', where the authors propose a deliberate, systematic explanation (misalignment of oracle reward design for image-based learning). The intended word was likely 'unexpectedly' or 'surprisingly'. Using 'by chance' creates a logical tension between the observed result and the authors' proposed mechanism, weakening the analytical claim.

comments.title: Coordinate convention mismatch with standard image processing omitted
comments.quote: The first set (left of, right of, on top of, below, and in between) assume coordinates in a front camera view and the semantics are defined in that camera view with positive y in the upward direction and positive x in the right direction.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly define the y-axis as pointing upward. However, standard image pixel coordinates (and consequently coordinates extracted via CLIP's ResNet backbone and Grad-CAM) have the y-axis pointing downward. The paper defines this coordinate convention but omits the crucial implementation detail of whether and how the extracted saliency centroids are transformed (e.g., y_flipped = H - y) before applying the spatial heuristics. While the convention is noted, the omission of the transformation step creates a reproducibility gap, as a reader would naturally assume pixel coordinates are fed directly into the equations.

comments.title: Underspecified embedding mechanism for primitive code baseline
comments.quote: Since it only has primitive codes for the training tasks it uses CLIP’s language embedding to find the closest primitive code it was trained with to the test language goal description.
comments.explanation: The baseline assigns discrete integer labels (0-17) to training tasks. The text states that CLIP's language embedding is used to match test descriptions to these codes, but it omits how the integers are represented in the embedding space. CLIP does not natively understand raw integers as meaningful text embeddings without tokenization (e.g., formatted as 'task 0', 'task 1') or a learned projection layer. Without specifying the tokenization or embedding strategy, a reader cannot accurately reproduce the primitive code conditioning baseline.

comments.title: Ambiguous Architecture Description for Multi-Task Policy
comments.quote: We train the multitask policy with ResNet18 image encoding that is concatenated with CLIP language encoding both of which are pushed through an fc layer before concatenation.
comments.explanation: Initially, this sentence is confusing due to contradictory syntactic cues. It claims the encodings are concatenated, but then states 'both of which are pushed through an fc layer before concatenation.' I checked standard multimodal fusion pipelines and the surrounding context, which strongly suggests the authors intended for each modality's output to be passed through a separate fully connected layer *prior* to concatenation. However, the current phrasing leaves the exact sequence of operations (concatenate then FC vs. FC then concatenate) ambiguous. Since architectural details are crucial for reproducibility, this requires clarification. Type: technical, Severity: moderate.

comments.title: Questionable 'Surprise' Claim Regarding Conditioning Methods
comments.quote: Surprisingly language conditioning performs significantly better than primitive code conditioning in training task performance.
comments.explanation: Upon reading this, I expected language conditioning to outperform discrete primitive codes, as natural language embeddings provide a dense, semantically rich, and continuous representation compared to sparse integer IDs. The authors label this difference as 'surprising,' which seems to overstate the novelty of the result. While the empirical gap is valuable, the direction of improvement is theoretically expected given the representational capacity of the conditioning signals. This is a minor framing concern that could be toned down to match standard expectations in the field. Type: logical, Severity: minor.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Oracle Reward Specification for Evaluation
comments.quote: We use the same oracle reward that has direct access to state in figure 6 to measure the performance of our multi-task models. Every timestep the objects are in the correct target text description the agent is rewarded 1 point.
comments.explanation: The authors state they use the oracle reward from Figure 6 to measure multi-task model performance, specifying that 1 point is awarded per timestep the goal state is met. I verified this against Section 3.2, which defines task-specific oracle rewards (e.g., distance/velocity penalties for pendulum, sparse triggers for manipulation). The current passage doesn't explicitly clarify that this uniform 1-point-per-timestep rule is an *evaluation* metric applied uniformly across tasks, rather than the reward signal used during RL training. Without this distinction, a careful reader might incorrectly assume the policies were trained with this simplified dense reward structure. Clarifying its role strictly as an evaluation metric would resolve this. Type: technical, Severity: minor.

comments.title: Garbled/Contradictory Statement Regarding State Specification
comments.quote: Our current method also does not specify state of the system other than semantic position such as object pose or closed door.
comments.explanation: This sentence appears abruptly before the ethics paragraph in the conclusion/draft end-matter. It is syntactically awkward and semantically unclear. Given the paper's explicit and repeated premise (Sections 1, 2, and Figure 1 captions) that the framework operates with 'no access to the underlying state of the environment', claiming the method 'does not specify state... other than semantic position' is confusing and risks contradicting the zero-state assumption. It likely contains a typo (e.g., intended 'does not require access to state') or is a misplaced sentence from a limitations or discussion draft. Without clarification, readers may be unsure whether the reward model implicitly conditions on privileged state variables beyond the defined semantic positions.

comments.title: Coordinate Convention Assumption for Spatial Heuristics
comments.quote: The first set (left of, right of, on top of, below, and in between) assume coordinates in a front camera view and the semantics are defined in that camera view with positive y in the upward direction and positive x in the right direction.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly define a custom coordinate system where y increases upward to align with intuitive spatial relationships ('on top' implies higher y). However, this contradicts the standard computer vision convention where image pixel coordinates are measured with y increasing downward. Table 1 lists the spatial criteria without restating or symbolically reflecting this inverted y-convention. A reproducing researcher assuming standard image coordinates will likely implement the 'on top' and 'below' heuristics backwards. The table or notation should explicitly denote the coordinate transformation (e.g., defining $O_{iy}$ as vertical height or noting $y_{	ext{image}} = H - y_{	ext{semantic}}$) to prevent implementation errors.

comments.title: Inconsistent Spatial Logic in Table 1
comments.quote: Obj1 on the left of Obj2 ... _x > O_1
comments.explanation: The passage presents a table of spatial grounding heuristics. The caption preceding the table states that for the first set of heuristics, "positive x in the right direction." For the relationship "Obj1 on the left of Obj2," this logically requires that the x-coordinate of Obj1 is less than the x-coordinate of Obj2 (i.e., $x_1 < x_2$). However, the row text corresponding to this relationship in the provided passage displays `_x > O_1`. Even considering potential OCR errors, this presents a direct logical contradiction to the coordinate system definition provided immediately above. While the formula itself is likely correct in the source paper (e.g., $x_1 < x_2$), the text in this passage renders the mathematical condition incorrectly.

comments.title: Under-specified Object State Extraction Method
comments.quote: The state extractor in Figure 2 computes the 'state' of each object using the argmax of the saliency heatmap.
comments.explanation: The text describes extracting the 'state' of *each* object (plural) from a saliency heatmap. A saliency heatmap (e.g., from Grad-CAM) is a spatial activation map (2D). Taking an argmax of this map yields a single point $(x, y)$, which corresponds to the location of only one (typically the most dominant) object. To obtain the spatial state for multiple grounded objects simultaneously, one would need to perform a weighted average of coordinates or find multiple peaks. Using a single argmax seems insufficient to ground 'each' object mentioned in the task description (noun phrases $g_{o_1}...g_{o_k}$).

comments.title: Ambiguous variation in starting point location
comments.quote: In RL training and in policy rollout collection for behavior cloning each object has a one block width of variation in starting point location.
comments.explanation: The passage specifies that objects are initialized with a variation 'one block width' during training and rollout collection. However, the paper has not defined what 'block width' constitutes in terms of the physical coordinate system (e.g., is it the diameter, the radius, or a specific distance along the x-axis or y-axis?). This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand the effective noise added to the initial state distribution or to replicate the exact experimental setup described.

comments.title: Multiple Typographical and Formatting Errors
comments.quote: unseen intial state distribution; The policy is trained using Adam optimizer with AMS grad with learning rate of 1e-4; We encourage pursing the integration of these approaches to the grounding of spatial prepositions
comments.explanation: The text contains several typographical errors that should be corrected: 'intial' instead of 'initial' (paragraph 49), 'AMS grad' instead of 'AMSGrad' (paragraph 50), and 'pursing' instead of 'pursuing' (paragraph 57). While these are minor, they detract from the professionalism and technical clarity of the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistent Spatial Logic in Table 1
comments.quote: Obj1 on the left of Obj2 ... _x > O_1
comments.explanation: The passage presents a table of spatial grounding heuristics. The caption preceding the table states that for the first set of heuristics, "positive x in the right direction." For the relationship "Obj1 on the left of Obj2," this logically requires that the x-coordinate of Obj1 is less than the x-coordinate of Obj2 (i.e., $x_1 < x_2$). However, the row text corresponding to this relationship in the provided passage displays `_x > O_1`. Even considering potential OCR errors, this presents a direct logical contradiction to the coordinate system definition provided immediately above. While the formula itself is likely correct in the source paper (e.g., $x_1 < x_2$), the text in this passage renders the mathematical condition incorrectly.

comments.title: Under-specified Object State Extraction Method
comments.quote: The state extractor in Figure 2 computes the 'state' of each object using the argmax of the saliency heatmap.
comments.explanation: The text describes extracting the 'state' of *each* object (plural) from a saliency heatmap. A saliency heatmap (e.g., from Grad-CAM) is a spatial activation map (2D). Taking an argmax of this map yields a single point $(x, y)$, which corresponds to the location of only one (typically the most dominant) object. To obtain the spatial state for multiple grounded objects simultaneously, one would need to perform a weighted average of coordinates or find multiple peaks. Using a single argmax seems insufficient to ground 'each' object mentioned in the task description (noun phrases $g_{o_1}...g_{o_k}$).

comments.title: Ambiguous variation in starting point location
comments.quote: In RL training and in policy rollout collection for behavior cloning each object has a one block width of variation in starting point location.
comments.explanation: The passage specifies that objects are initialized with a variation 'one block width' during training and rollout collection. However, the paper has not defined what 'block width' constitutes in terms of the physical coordinate system (e.g., is it the diameter, the radius, or a specific distance along the x-axis or y-axis?). This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand the effective noise added to the initial state distribution or to replicate the exact experimental setup described.

comments.title: Typo in optimizer name
comments.quote: The policy is trained using Adam optimizer with AMS grad with learning rate of 1e-4.
comments.explanation: The optimizer name appears as 'AMS grad' (with a space) rather than 'AMSGrad' (standard convention). While this is likely a typographical error or OCR artifact, it is not standard terminology and should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Typo in distribution name
comments.quote: unseen intial state distribution
comments.explanation: The text contains a clear typo 'intial' instead of 'initial'. This appears to be an OCR error or simple spelling mistake in the version provided.

comments.title: Typo in future work direction
comments.quote: We encourage pursing the integration of these approaches to the grounding of spatial prepositions
comments.explanation: The word 'pursing' appears to be a spelling error for 'pursuing'. In the context of discussing future work and integrating approaches, the authors almost certainly intended to say 'pursuing'. This is a minor grammatical error that does not affect the technical understanding of the text but reduces the quality of the prose.
